title: AI Governance for PMs
description: Learn to build governance frameworks that enable responsible AI deployment — from risk assessment to compliance requirements to ethical considerations.
learningObjectives: Design an AI governance framework appropriate for your organization's size, industry, and risk tolerance
Build an AI review process that catches critical issues without becoming a bureaucratic bottleneck
Navigate data privacy, compliance, and bias considerations when planning AI features

sections.title: AI Governance Fundamentals for PMs
sections.content:
AI governance sounds like something for legal and compliance teams, not product managers. In reality, PMs are the most important people in AI governance because they make the day-to-day decisions about what AI features get built, how they behave, and what safeguards are in place.

**What AI Governance Actually Means**

AI governance is the set of policies, processes, and practices that ensure AI features are developed and deployed responsibly. For a PM, this translates into a series of concrete decisions that you're already making, whether you frame them as governance or not.

Consider **data flow decisions**. Every piece of user data sent to Claude is a governance decision. What gets included in the prompt, what gets stored, and what gets discarded are product decisions with governance implications. Similarly, the **behavioral boundaries** you set in system prompts — the topics you allow or disallow, the actions you permit the AI to take on behalf of users — define what the system can and cannot do. These boundaries are governance in action. Then there's the question of **human oversight**: whether outputs go directly to users or pass through human review is both a product design and governance decision. And finally, your approach to **failure handling** — who gets notified when the AI produces an error, how quickly you respond, what you communicate to affected users — is itself a governance process.

**Why PMs Should Care**

PMs who treat governance as an afterthought face predictable consequences. The most immediate is **delayed launches**: legal and compliance reviews that happen at the end of a project routinely add 2-4 weeks to timelines, and starting governance conversations early avoids these last-minute surprises. Beyond timelines, there's **reputational damage** to consider — an AI feature that produces biased, offensive, or incorrect outputs generates headlines and erodes user trust. Governance prevents these outcomes before they happen.

The stakes extend further. **Regulatory exposure** is growing as regulators in the EU, US, and other markets actively develop AI-specific regulations. Features built without governance in mind may need expensive retrofitting to comply. On the positive side, PMs who proactively address governance concerns build **internal credibility**, earning trust from legal, security, and leadership teams. That trust translates into faster approvals for future AI initiatives.

**The Governance Maturity Spectrum**

Not every organization needs the same level of governance. The key is matching your approach to your context, and understanding when it's time to level up.

At **Level 1 (Ad Hoc)**, AI features are reviewed case-by-case, with the PM and engineering lead making governance decisions together. This is appropriate for early-stage startups shipping their first AI feature. **Level 2 (Documented)** introduces written guidelines for AI features, including a checklist PMs complete before launch. This suits growing companies with multiple AI features in development. At **Level 3 (Systematic)**, an AI review board evaluates all AI features against established criteria, and data privacy impact assessments become mandatory — the right approach for companies in regulated industries or with significant AI exposure. Finally, **Level 4 (Proactive)** involves dedicated AI ethics resources, red-teaming exercises, ongoing monitoring of deployed AI features, and regular governance audits, appropriate for companies where AI is core to the product and errors carry high stakes.

Most companies should aim for Level 2 initially and progress to Level 3 as their AI portfolio grows.

**Using Claude to Bootstrap Your Governance Framework**

One of the most practical ways to accelerate governance work is to use Claude itself as a drafting partner. Rather than starting from a blank page, you can ask Claude to generate a first-draft governance checklist tailored to your industry, company size, and risk profile. Feed it your product context — the types of AI features you're building, the data you handle, the regulations that apply — and ask it to produce a checklist you can iterate on with legal and compliance stakeholders. Claude can also help you draft policy language for AI-acceptable-use policies, data handling procedures, and incident response plans. The output won't be legally binding on its own, but it gives you a structured starting point that's far more productive than assembling a framework from scratch. PMs who use Claude this way often cut the time from 'no governance' to 'documented Level 2 framework' from weeks to days.

playground.description: Try using Claude to draft a governance checklist tailored to a specific company profile. Experiment with changing the industry, company size, or risk factors to see how the framework adapts.
playground.systemPrompt: You are an AI governance advisor helping product managers build practical governance frameworks. Generate actionable, specific governance checklists tailored to the company context provided. Focus on concrete items a PM can act on — avoid generic advice. Structure your output with clear risk tiers, specific checklist items, and responsible parties. Keep the framework lightweight enough to actually be adopted.
playground.userMessage: I'm a PM at a Series B healthtech startup (80 employees, 50 clinic customers). We're launching our first AI feature: Claude will summarize patient visit notes for clinic administrators. The summaries help admins track care quality but don't inform clinical decisions. Draft a governance checklist I can bring to our first AI review meeting with legal and engineering.

sections.title: Building an AI Review Process
sections.content:
An AI review process ensures that AI features are evaluated for risk, quality, and compliance before they reach users. The challenge is building a process that catches real issues without becoming a bureaucratic bottleneck that slows down every AI initiative.

**The AI Feature Review Checklist**

Every AI feature should answer a core set of questions before launch, with the depth of each answer scaling to the feature's risk level. Think of these as the six dimensions of AI readiness:

• **Data inventory:** What user data does this feature send to Claude? Is any of it classified (PII, financial, health)? Is the handling consistent with your privacy policy and user consent?
• **Output controls:** What guardrails prevent harmful, offensive, or misleading content? Have you tested adversarial inputs? Are system prompt boundaries clearly defined?
• **Human oversight:** At what point does a human review Claude's output? Is the human-in-the-loop mechanism genuinely effective, or is it a rubber stamp?
• **Failure handling:** What happens when Claude produces a bad output? How is the error detected, logged, and resolved? Who gets notified?
• **Monitoring plan:** How will you track output quality after launch? What metrics trigger an alert? Who is on call for AI-related incidents?
• **User transparency:** Does the user know they're interacting with AI? Can they opt out? Can they report issues?

The value of this checklist is that it forces you to think through each dimension explicitly rather than assuming someone else has it covered.

**Risk-Tiered Review**

Not every AI feature needs the same level of scrutiny. Classifying features into risk tiers lets you scale your review process appropriately.

**Low-risk** features include internal tools, non-customer-facing features, or features where the AI assists but doesn't decide — for example, Claude suggesting tags for internal documents. These need only a PM and engineering lead sign-off. **Medium-risk** features are customer-facing and generate content that users see, but with human review built in. An example would be Claude drafting customer emails that agents review before sending. These require PM and engineering lead approval plus a legal review of customer-facing language. **High-risk** features are those where AI outputs directly affect decisions about people, money, or safety — think Claude scoring insurance claims or prioritizing support tickets by severity. These warrant a full AI review board including PM, engineering, legal, data science, and a domain expert.

**Avoiding Review Bottlenecks**

The biggest risk of a governance process is that it becomes a gate that blocks all AI development. Preventing this requires deliberate process design.

The most important step is making **low-risk reviews self-serve**. Create a checklist that PMs can complete independently, and only escalate to formal review when the checklist flags a concern. Beyond that, **run reviews in parallel with development** rather than waiting until the feature is built. Beginning the governance conversation during the planning phase ensures that issues surface early, when they're cheapest to address. You should also **set SLAs for reviews** — legal and compliance teams should commit to review timelines, because a governance review that takes six weeks with no visibility into progress is broken. Finally, **document precedents** whenever a review board makes a decision. Recording the reasoning allows future features with similar profiles to reference the precedent instead of starting from scratch.

**Claude as Your Review Process Copilot**

Claude can dramatically speed up the review process itself. Before a formal review meeting, you can have Claude pre-populate a risk assessment by describing your feature and asking it to identify potential data privacy, bias, and compliance concerns across each dimension of the checklist. Claude can also review draft system prompts for gaps in output controls or missing guardrails — feed it the prompt and ask it to red-team the boundaries. After a review board meeting, Claude can help you turn discussion notes into structured decision documents with clear rationale, action items, and precedent language that future PMs can reference. This doesn't replace human judgment on governance decisions, but it eliminates the blank-page problem and ensures that review artifacts are thorough and well-organized.

sections.title: Data Privacy and Compliance
sections.content:
Every AI feature has a data story — what data goes in, what comes out, and where it flows in between. As a PM, you don't need to be a privacy lawyer, but you need to understand the basics well enough to make informed decisions and ask the right questions.

**Data Classification for AI Features**

Before building any AI feature, classify the data it will process. This classification determines which regulations apply and what level of governance the feature requires. Data sensitivity exists on a spectrum, and each level carries different implications for your governance approach:

• **Public data** — information already publicly available, like published product documentation. Lowest risk.
• **Internal data** — company data that isn't public but doesn't contain personal information, like sales reports. Risk centers on competitive sensitivity and data leakage.
• **Personal data (PII)** — any data that identifies or could identify a specific individual: names, email addresses, phone numbers, user IDs. Triggers privacy regulation requirements.
• **Sensitive personal data** — health information, financial data, biometric data, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, sexual orientation. Requires the strongest governance controls and often explicit user consent.

Knowing where your feature falls on this spectrum is the first question to answer, because it shapes every governance decision that follows.

**Key Regulatory Frameworks**

You don't need to memorize regulations, but you do need to know which ones might apply to your AI feature so you can involve the right people at the right time.

**GDPR** applies if you process data of EU or EEA residents. Its key requirements include establishing a lawful basis for processing, practicing data minimization (only sending Claude what it needs), honoring the right to explanation (users can ask how automated decisions were made), and maintaining data processing agreements with API providers. **CCPA/CPRA** is similar to GDPR but includes specific requirements around the sale and sharing of personal information — if Claude processes data that's later used for personalization or advertising, CCPA may apply.

**SOC 2** is not a regulation but a trust standard that many B2B customers require. If your customers ask for SOC 2 compliance, your AI data flows are in scope, and you'll need to document what data goes to Claude and how it's handled.

Finally, various **industry-specific regulations** may apply depending on your product's domain:

• **HIPAA** for healthcare
• **FERPA** for education
• **FINRA/SEC** for financial services
• **PCI DSS** for payment data

If your product touches any of these industries, AI data flows need to comply with the relevant industry-specific rules. The specifics vary, but the common thread is that these regulations impose stricter requirements on how data is processed, stored, and audited — all of which your AI feature must satisfy.

**Practical Privacy Decisions for PMs**

Beyond understanding the regulatory landscape, there are specific privacy decisions you'll face repeatedly when building AI features.

The first is **what to include in prompts**. The guiding principle is data minimization: send Claude the minimum data necessary to produce a good output. If you're summarizing a support ticket, ask whether you actually need the customer's name and email, or whether you can anonymize them. Minimizing data reduces risk across the board. Closely related is **data retention** — whether your application stores Claude's responses, for how long, and whether users can request deletion. Build data lifecycle management into your feature design from the start.

**User consent** is a common blocker that PMs discover too late. Does your existing privacy policy cover sending user data to an AI API? If not, you may need to update it and obtain new consent before launching. **Cross-border data transfer** presents another challenge: if your users are in the EU and Claude's API processes data in the US, you may need additional data protection safeguards, so check with legal early. For regulated industries, you'll also need an **audit trail** that logs what data was sent to Claude, what was returned, and what action was taken. Design this logging into the feature architecture from the beginning, not as an afterthought.

**Drafting Compliance Documentation with Claude**

Compliance documentation is one of the highest-leverage places to use Claude as a PM tool. You can provide Claude with your feature's data flow — what user data enters the system, where it's processed, how long it's retained — and ask it to generate a first-draft data privacy impact assessment. Claude can also help you draft the privacy-related sections of vendor security questionnaires, which B2B customers routinely send and which consume hours of PM and legal time. For consent management, you can describe your current privacy policy and the new AI feature's data practices, then ask Claude to identify gaps where your policy may need updating. The result is a focused list of policy changes to discuss with legal rather than a vague 'we should probably update our privacy policy' conversation.

playground.description: Practice using Claude to draft a data privacy impact assessment for a realistic AI feature. See how specifying different data types and regulations changes the output.
playground.systemPrompt: You are a privacy and compliance advisor helping product managers assess data privacy implications of AI features. When given a feature description, produce a structured data privacy impact assessment covering: data classification, applicable regulations, consent requirements, data minimization recommendations, retention policy suggestions, and cross-border transfer considerations. Be specific to the feature described — avoid generic boilerplate. Flag the highest-priority items the PM should discuss with legal.
playground.userMessage: Our B2B SaaS platform serves enterprise customers in the US and EU. We want to add an AI feature where Claude analyzes customer support ticket history (which contains customer names, email addresses, account details, and free-text issue descriptions) to generate weekly trend reports for account managers. The reports will be stored in our database and visible in the account manager dashboard. Draft a data privacy impact assessment I can review with our legal team.

sections.title: Bias Detection and Mitigation
sections.content:
AI bias is not a theoretical concern — it's a practical product risk. Claude, like all language models, can produce outputs that reflect or amplify biases present in its training data. As a PM, you need to understand where bias can appear, how to detect it, and what to do about it.

**Where Bias Appears in AI Features**

Bias can enter your AI feature at multiple points in the pipeline, and understanding these entry points is the first step toward addressing them.

**Input bias** occurs when the data you send to Claude is itself skewed. If your customer support training data over-represents certain demographics, Claude's responses may be subtly optimized for those groups at the expense of others. **Prompt bias** is introduced through the examples, instructions, and framing in your prompts — if your few-shot examples all feature male professionals in technical roles, Claude may default to that framing in its outputs. Even with neutral inputs and carefully constructed prompts, **output bias** can still appear, manifesting as different tones, different levels of formality, or different assumptions about users based on names or other identity signals. Finally, **evaluation bias** can hide problems from view entirely: if your test set doesn't include diverse inputs, you simply won't catch biased outputs during testing.

**Detecting Bias**

Bias detection for AI features doesn't require a PhD in machine learning. What it does require is systematic testing with diverse inputs and a willingness to look for patterns you might prefer not to find.

**Demographic parity testing** is the most straightforward approach: run the same query with different names, locations, or demographic signals and compare the outputs. Does Claude's product recommendation change when the user's name changes? Does the tone of a support response differ based on implied gender? These comparisons often reveal subtle disparities that aren't visible in individual outputs.

When your AI feature makes decisions like prioritization, categorization, or scoring, **outcome distribution analysis** becomes essential. Analyze the distribution of outcomes across demographic groups and look for patterns — are certain groups consistently scored lower, categorized differently, or deprioritized?

**Red-teaming for bias** adds a human dimension to the process. Dedicate specific testing sessions to probing for bias, and have team members from diverse backgrounds interact with the feature and report instances where outputs feel biased, unfair, or uncomfortable. After launch, **user feedback analysis** extends this practice by segmenting user satisfaction data by demographics where available and appropriate. Disparities in satisfaction may indicate bias in the AI feature's behavior that testing didn't catch.

**Mitigating Bias**

Once you've identified bias, you have several tools to address it, and the most effective strategies typically combine multiple approaches.

**Prompt engineering** is often the first line of defense. Explicitly instruct Claude to treat all users equally, avoid assumptions based on names or demographics, and use neutral language. Including diverse examples in few-shot prompts helps reinforce these instructions through demonstration. **Input preprocessing** takes a complementary approach by removing or anonymizing demographic signals from the data sent to Claude when those signals aren't relevant to the task — if Claude doesn't need the user's name to generate a report, don't include it.

On the output side, **post-processing** adds a review step that checks outputs for biased language or disparate treatment before they reach users. This can be automated for certain types of bias, such as gendered language, and manual for subtler cases. Underpinning all of these techniques, **diverse evaluation** ensures you have an evaluation set that includes inputs from diverse demographic groups, geographies, and use cases, tested against with every prompt change. And because bias can emerge over time as usage patterns shift, **ongoing monitoring** that tracks output distributions and flags anomalies for review is essential even after you've addressed initial findings.

**The Fairness vs. Performance Tradeoff**

Sometimes bias mitigation affects output quality. Removing a user's name from the prompt might reduce personalization quality. Using strictly neutral language might make outputs feel generic. These are real tradeoffs that PMs need to make deliberately, not accidentally. Document your decisions and the reasoning behind them so that future teams understand why certain tradeoffs were made and can revisit them as capabilities and norms evolve.

sections.title: Design an AI Governance Checklist
sections.content: Build a practical governance checklist that a PM at your organization could use to evaluate any AI feature before launch.

exercise.prompt:
You're the PM responsible for establishing AI governance practices at a mid-stage B2B SaaS company (200 employees, Series C, 500 enterprise customers). The company is launching its first three AI features this quarter and currently has no AI governance framework. Leadership has asked you to create a practical governance checklist.

Design an AI governance checklist that covers:

1. **Risk Classification** — Define 3 risk tiers (low, medium, high) with specific criteria for classifying AI features into each tier. Include at least 2 concrete examples of features that would fall into each tier.
2. **Pre-Launch Checklist** — Create a checklist of 8-10 items that must be completed before any AI feature launches. Each item should be specific and actionable (not vague like "consider privacy"). For each item, note which risk tiers require it.
3. **Data Privacy Section** — Identify the 5 most important privacy questions a PM should answer for any AI feature. For each question, explain why it matters and what a good answer looks like.
4. **Bias Assessment** — Describe a practical bias testing protocol that a small team (no dedicated ML ethics team) can execute in 2-3 days. Include specific test types, sample sizes, and decision criteria.
5. **Post-Launch Monitoring** — Define what ongoing monitoring looks like after an AI feature ships. What metrics do you track, how often, and what triggers an escalation?
hints: Risk tiers should be based on factors like: Does the AI output reach customers directly? Does it influence decisions about people? Does it process sensitive data? Could errors cause financial or reputational harm?
The checklist should be practical enough that a PM can complete it in 1-2 hours for a low-risk feature. If it takes a week, nobody will use it.

For bias testing, think about what a small team can realistically do: swap demographic signals in inputs, compare outputs across groups, and spot-check with diverse reviewers. You don't need a statistical framework — you need a repeatable process.
Post-launch monitoring should include both automated alerts (error rate spikes, acceptance rate drops) and periodic human reviews (monthly output quality audits)
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) Three clearly differentiated risk tiers with specific, objective classification criteria — not subjective judgments like 'feels risky.' Each tier should have 2+ concrete feature examples that demonstrate the classification logic. (2) A pre-launch checklist of 8-10 specific, actionable items (not vague categories) with risk-tier applicability marked — items should be completable and verifiable, not open-ended. (3) Five privacy questions with explanations of why each matters and what constitutes a good answer — generic questions like 'is there a privacy risk' without specifics are insufficient. (4) A bias testing protocol achievable by a small team in 2-3 days with specific test types, realistic scope, and clear decision criteria for pass/fail — protocols that require dedicated ML ethics expertise or months of work miss the practical constraint. (5) A post-launch monitoring plan with specific metrics, frequencies, and escalation triggers — 'monitor quality' without specifying what, how often, and what threshold triggers action is too vague. Deduct points for checklists that are too abstract to use in practice, privacy sections that don't address data flow through the AI API, or bias protocols that aren't feasible for a small team.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content:
Before moving on, make sure you can answer these questions:

• What's the difference between a Level 2 and Level 3 governance maturity organization, and when should a company move from one to the other?
• Why should AI governance conversations start during project planning rather than before launch?
• What's the most common privacy mistake PMs make when building AI features, and how do you avoid it?
• How would you explain the business value of bias testing to a skeptical engineering lead?

exercise.prompt:
Your company (a mid-size fintech serving small business lenders) is about to launch an AI feature where Claude reviews loan applications and generates risk summary reports for human underwriters. The reports include a risk score rationale, flagged concerns, and a recommended next step. The underwriter makes the final decision, but the AI summary heavily influences their judgment.

Write a governance brief (1-2 pages) that covers:

1. **Risk classification** — What risk tier does this feature belong to and why? Reference specific factors from the governance maturity framework.
2. **Top 3 governance risks** — Identify the three most critical governance risks for this specific feature. For each risk, explain the potential impact and a concrete mitigation strategy.
3. **Data privacy plan** — What data flows through this feature, how should it be classified, and what privacy safeguards are needed? Address regulatory considerations specific to fintech.
4. **Bias testing plan** — Outline a practical bias testing approach for loan application summaries. What demographic dimensions matter most, and what would a 'fail' look like?
5. **Stakeholder communication** — Draft a brief pitch (3-4 sentences) explaining to your CEO why this feature needs governance review before launch, framed in terms of business risk rather than compliance obligation.
evaluationCriteria: Correctly classifies the feature as high-risk with specific justification referencing decision influence, financial impact, and regulatory exposure — not just a generic label
Identifies governance risks specific to AI-assisted lending (fair lending laws, disparate impact, explainability requirements) rather than generic AI risks
Data privacy plan addresses fintech-specific regulations and covers the full data lifecycle from application intake through Claude processing to report storage
Bias testing plan focuses on protected classes relevant to lending (race, gender, age, geography) with concrete test methodology and clear pass/fail criteria
CEO pitch frames governance as business risk mitigation and competitive advantage rather than compliance burden

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
AI governance is a PM responsibility, not just a legal or compliance function. Building governance into your AI development process protects your users, your company, and your career.

**Governance is a product decision.** The data that flows through your AI, the boundaries the system operates within, and the way failures are handled are all product decisions with governance implications. They don't belong to legal or compliance by default — they belong to you, and owning them proactively is what separates strong AI PMs from reactive ones.

**Scale governance to risk.** A low-risk internal tool needs a PM checklist; a high-risk customer-facing decision system needs a review board. Matching the process to the stakes prevents two equally harmful outcomes: moving so slowly that AI initiatives stall, or moving so fast that critical issues slip through.

**Start privacy conversations early.** The most common governance failure is discovering a privacy or compliance issue two weeks before launch. By beginning data classification and legal review during the planning phase, you avoid the painful last-minute scramble that delays launches and frustrates teams.

**Bias testing is practical, not academic.** You don't need a dedicated ethics team or months of research to test for bias. Swapping demographic signals in inputs, comparing outputs across groups, and including diverse reviewers in your evaluation process can be accomplished in days and will catch the most significant issues.

**Monitor after launch.** Governance doesn't end when the feature ships. AI features can degrade, develop new failure modes, and encounter inputs that weren't anticipated during development. Building ongoing monitoring into your feature's operational plan ensures that governance remains a living practice, not a one-time checkbox.
